[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Target Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Target Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Target Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>LABEL</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

Represent the classification evidence using minimal and concise Description Logic statements.

# WHAT TO WRITE
- In <tbox>, define the formal class conditions (Necessary, Sufficient, or Necessary & Sufficient) using Description Logic (DL) axioms derived from the labeled examples to describe which visual features are associated with a class.
- In <abox>, state only the specific property assertions observed in the Target Image to record which visual features are present.
- In <dl_explanation>, explain exactly how the specific assertions in the ABox satisfy the formal axioms in the TBox to correctly classify the Target Image.

# HOW TO INTERPRET THE STATEMENTS
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([Feature]) means: images of this class tend to have this visual feature in the provided examples (necessary condition)
- hasVisualFeature([Feature]) sqsubseteq [Class] means: observing this visual feature is enough to classify the image as from this class in the provided examples (sufficient condition)
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2]) means: this combination of features defines this class in the provided examples (necessary and sufficient condition)

# RULES
- Use only observable visual features.
- Do not use hidden properties, external knowledge, or speculation.
- Treat all axioms as visually grounded in the provided examples.
- Keep the output concise.

<tbox>
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([F])
- hasVisualFeature([F]) sqsubseteq [Class]
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2])
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
- The Target Image contains the properties asserted in the ABox.
- Explain how these ABox assertions satisfy the TBox axioms.
- Conclude why [Class] is the logically derived label based on that explanation.
</dl_explanation>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<tbox>
- Class 81 ⊑ hasVisualFeature(bright pink/purple star-shaped flower with 6-8 broad pointed petals, central clustered whitish stamens, green foliage background)
- hasVisualFeature(bright pink/purple star-shaped flower with 6-8 broad pointed petals, central clustered whitish stamens, green foliage background) ⊑ Class 81
- Class 14 ⊑ hasVisualFeature(yellow iris-style elongated curved petaled flower, single central bloom, grassy green foliage, purple-toned background)
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, bright pink/purple star-shaped flower with 6-8 broad pointed petals, central clustered whitish stamens, green foliage background)
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
The Target Image exhibits all the formal visual features defined for Class 81: it has pink-purple star-shaped flowers with broad pointed petals, a central cluster of whitish stamens, and green leafy foliage as the background. These assertions fully satisfy the necessary and sufficient conditions for Class 81 from the TBox, so 81 is the correct classification.
</dl_explanation>
<response>81</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Target Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Target Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Target Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>LABEL</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

Represent the classification evidence using minimal and concise Description Logic statements.

# WHAT TO WRITE
- In <tbox>, define the formal class conditions (Necessary, Sufficient, or Necessary & Sufficient) using Description Logic (DL) axioms derived from the labeled examples to describe which visual features are associated with a class.
- In <abox>, state only the specific property assertions observed in the Target Image to record which visual features are present.
- In <dl_explanation>, explain exactly how the specific assertions in the ABox satisfy the formal axioms in the TBox to correctly classify the Target Image.

# HOW TO INTERPRET THE STATEMENTS
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([Feature]) means: images of this class tend to have this visual feature in the provided examples (necessary condition)
- hasVisualFeature([Feature]) sqsubseteq [Class] means: observing this visual feature is enough to classify the image as from this class in the provided examples (sufficient condition)
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2]) means: this combination of features defines this class in the provided examples (necessary and sufficient condition)

# RULES
- Use only observable visual features.
- Do not use hidden properties, external knowledge, or speculation.
- Treat all axioms as visually grounded in the provided examples.
- Keep the output concise.

<tbox>
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([F])
- hasVisualFeature([F]) sqsubseteq [Class]
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2])
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
- The Target Image contains the properties asserted in the ABox.
- Explain how these ABox assertions satisfy the TBox axioms.
- Conclude why [Class] is the logically derived label based on that explanation.
</dl_explanation>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

message.reasoning:
Got it, let's work through this step by step. First, the tbox: first, look at the examples. Label 81 is the purple/pink clematis flowers with the pointed petal (star-shaped, 5-8 pointed petals, pink/purple color, with clustered stamens in the center). Label 14 is the yellow iris flowers with the elongated, pointed yellow blooms, different structure.

Wait the target image has pink/purple flowers with multiple broad, pointed petals, central stamen cluster, that's the same as the earlier 81 examples. Let's structure the tbox properly:

First, <tbox>
- Class 81 sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature(bright pink/purple star-shaped flower with 6-8 broad pointed petals, central clustered whitish stamens, green foliage background)
- hasVisualFeature(bright pink/purple star-shaped flower with 6-8 broad pointed petals, central clustered whitish stamens, green foliage background) sqsubseteq Class 81
- Class 14 sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature(yellow iris-style flower with elongated curved petals, single central bloom, green grassy foliage, purple background)
</tbox>

Then <abox>:
- hasVisualFeature(Target, bright pink/purple star-shaped flower with 6-8 broad pointed petals, central clustered whitish stamens, green foliage background)
</abox>

Then the dl_explanation: The target image has the visual features defined for Class 81: it has bright pink-purple star-shaped flowers with multiple broad pointed petals, a central cluster of whitish stamens, and green leafy foliage in the background. This matches the necessary and sufficient conditions for Class 81, so the correct label is 81.

Then the response block with <response>81</response>

raw_response_text:
<tbox>
- [14] equiv hasVisualFeature(yellow iris-shaped flower petals) and hasVisualFeature(bright yellow petal color with dark vein striations) and hasVisualFeature(green leafy background foliage)
- [81] equiv hasVisualFeature(magenta/purple 4-6 pointed flower petals) and hasVisualFeature(light colored central floral stamen) and hasVisualFeature(green background foliage)
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, yellow iris-shaped flower petals)
- hasVisualFeature(Target, bright yellow petal color with dark vein striations)
- hasVisualFeature(Target, green leafy background foliage)
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
- The Target Image contains all the visual features specified in the necessary and sufficient conditions for class [14]. The target has iris-style yellow petals with veined bright yellow coloring and a green leafy out-of-focus background, which fully matches the axioms for [14]. These features do not align with the defining traits of [81], which are purple/magenta clematis flowers with light central stamens.
</dl_explanation>
<response>14</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Target Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Target Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Target Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>LABEL</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

Represent the classification evidence using minimal and concise Description Logic statements.

# WHAT TO WRITE
- In <tbox>, define the formal class conditions (Necessary, Sufficient, or Necessary & Sufficient) using Description Logic (DL) axioms derived from the labeled examples to describe which visual features are associated with a class.
- In <abox>, state only the specific property assertions observed in the Target Image to record which visual features are present.
- In <dl_explanation>, explain exactly how the specific assertions in the ABox satisfy the formal axioms in the TBox to correctly classify the Target Image.

# HOW TO INTERPRET THE STATEMENTS
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([Feature]) means: images of this class tend to have this visual feature in the provided examples (necessary condition)
- hasVisualFeature([Feature]) sqsubseteq [Class] means: observing this visual feature is enough to classify the image as from this class in the provided examples (sufficient condition)
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2]) means: this combination of features defines this class in the provided examples (necessary and sufficient condition)

# RULES
- Use only observable visual features.
- Do not use hidden properties, external knowledge, or speculation.
- Treat all axioms as visually grounded in the provided examples.
- Keep the output concise.

<tbox>
- [Class] sqsubseteq hasVisualFeature([F])
- hasVisualFeature([F]) sqsubseteq [Class]
- [Class] equiv hasVisualFeature([F1]) and hasVisualFeature([F2])
</tbox>
<abox>
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
- hasVisualFeature(Target, [F])
</abox>
<dl_explanation>
- The Target Image contains the properties asserted in the ABox.
- Explain how these ABox assertions satisfy the TBox axioms.
- Conclude why [Class] is the logically derived label based on that explanation.
</dl_explanation>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.
